query: A patient's CSF panel shows mildly elevated p-tau181 but the clinician notes the Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio is in the abnormal range; which finding is the primary indicator under the ATN framework?
rationale: T biomarker mentioned but A is the asked subject under ATN

query: Despite normal hippocampal volume on MRI, this patient shows markedly elevated p-tau217. What does the tau pathology suggest about the disease stage?
rationale: N biomarker mentioned as background, T is the focus

query: A 70-year-old with normal CSF Aβ42 but progressive hippocampal atrophy on serial MRI; what neurodegenerative process is most consistent with the imaging finding?
rationale: A biomarker as red herring (normal), N is the focus

query: In a patient with positive amyloid PET, plasma GFAP is also elevated. What does the glial response indicate about the neuroinflammatory component?
rationale: A as context, I is the asked subject

query: A patient carries APOE ε4/ε4 and shows mild hippocampal atrophy. What vascular-metabolic implications follow from the genotype?
rationale: N mentioned as comorbidity, V is the asked subject

query: Among p-tau181, p-tau217, NfL, and GFAP, which biomarker most directly reflects neurofibrillary tangle burden?
rationale: Multi-biomarker listing across axes; T is correct

query: A patient has elevated plasma NfL, normal p-tau, and reduced CSF Aβ42/Aβ40. Which axis is preferentially abnormal here in the AD-defining sense?
rationale: T and N as foils; only A is preferentially abnormal in AD-defining biomarker

query: When microglia and reactive astrocytes co-localize around amyloid plaques in postmortem AD brain, which axis does this pattern primarily represent?
rationale: A mentioned but I is the pattern

query: Patient's blood test shows elevated NfL after a recent fall. Does this finding still inform AD risk assessment, and through which axis?
rationale: NfL in trauma context still maps to N

query: A research paper reports increased CRP, IL-6, and YKL-40 in early MCI patients without overt cognitive symptoms. What axis is being characterized?
rationale: Multiple inflammatory markers, axis I

query: Discuss the implications of finding high HbA1c, elevated homocysteine, and prior stroke history in a 65-year-old presenting with cognitive complaints.
rationale: HbA1c + homocysteine + stroke = V

query: How does the staging system based on NFT distribution differ from the staging based on senile plaque distribution in postmortem AD diagnosis?
rationale: NFT = neurofibrillary tangle = T (Braak staging vs Thal staging)

query: TREM2 R47H variant carriers show altered microglial phagocytic function. What axis does this genetic-functional link characterize?
rationale: TREM2 in microglia context = I

query: FDG-PET in the precuneus and posterior cingulate cortex is reduced in this patient. What axis does this metabolic imaging finding reflect?
rationale: FDG-PET = neurodegeneration marker, axis N (per ATN); not metabolic-V despite the word 'metabolic'

query: BBB permeability measured by DCE-MRI is increased in APOE ε4 carriers before any cognitive decline. Which axis is implicated?
rationale: BBB + APOE = V

query: Serial brain imaging over 3 years shows progressive shrinkage of the medial temporal lobe. What pathological process does this longitudinal change reflect?
rationale: 'shrinkage' = atrophy = N, no explicit biomarker name

query: Postmortem analysis reveals dense, congophilic, β-pleated sheet deposits in the cerebral cortex of this AD patient. What axis is being described?
rationale: Description of amyloid plaque without saying 'amyloid'

query: Electron microscopy of cortical neurons shows paired helical filaments accumulating in the cell body and dendrites. Which axis does this ultrastructural finding represent?
rationale: PHF = tau, axis T

query: Histology shows ramified cells with thickened processes clustering around damaged neurons in AD cortex. Which axis is this response associated with?
rationale: Activated microglia description, axis I

query: Postmortem examination reveals multiple small infarcts, arteriolosclerosis, and leukoaraiosis throughout the periventricular white matter. Which axis is implicated?
rationale: Small vessel disease description, axis V

query: A patient's brain on autopsy weighs 1050 grams (normal age-adjusted: 1250 g) with conspicuous widening of the sulci. What axis does this gross finding represent?
rationale: Brain atrophy gross description, axis N

query: PiB-PET imaging in this 65-year-old cognitively normal adult shows widespread cortical retention. What axis is being assessed?
rationale: PiB is amyloid PET tracer; less common name than 'amyloid PET'

query: CSF analysis in this preclinical case shows elevated chitinase-3-like protein 1 alongside normal Aβ. What does the CSF profile suggest about the innate immune axis?
rationale: Chitinase-3-like protein 1 = YKL-40 full name; I axis

query: Mrs. K, 71, retired teacher with 14 years of education, came to memory clinic complaining of forgetting recent conversations. Her son drove her, she has hypertension on amlodipine and mild osteoarthritis. Plasma assay returned Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio of 0.045. MoCA was 24/30. Family history is unremarkable. Which biomarker axis is positive in this work-up?
rationale: Heavy noise (occupation, education, comorbidities, family), real signal is Aβ42/Aβ40

query: Mr. L, 68, presents for follow-up after his daughter noticed word-finding difficulty over 18 months. He plays tennis weekly, no smoking history, BMI 24. Routine labs unremarkable except plasma p-tau217 at 0.78 pg/mL (cutoff 0.40). Brain MRI shows mild generalized volume loss. What is the most informative AD biomarker axis here?
rationale: Multi-axis findings (T abnormal, N mildly abnormal); T is the most informative

query: Ms. M, 64, type 2 diabetes for 12 years, HbA1c 8.1%, current smoker, presents with subjective memory complaints. APOE ε3/ε4. BP 158/94. Cognitive testing within normal limits. Plasma p-tau and Aβ markers are normal. What axis dominates her AD risk profile?
rationale: A, T normal; V dominates via T2DM + smoking + HTN + APOE

query: Dr. R, 69 yo physician, complains of progressive difficulty recalling patient names over 2 years. CSF Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio is borderline-low, p-tau181 mildly elevated, but the most striking finding is bilateral hippocampal volume at 5th percentile for age on volumetric MRI. Which axis carries the strongest signal?
rationale: All three ATN abnormal but N has strongest signal (5th percentile)

query: A 72-year-old retired engineer with 20 years of education shows MoCA 26/30 and reports occasional word-finding pauses but no functional impairment. He asks about cognitive reserve and lifestyle factors. What is the primary topic of inquiry?
rationale: No biomarker, education + reserve + lifestyle = OTHER

query: Mrs. P, 75, presented to ER after a fall. CT was negative. During admission workup, plasma GFAP returned at 245 pg/mL (normal <150) and sTREM2 was also elevated. She was previously cognitively intact. What axis is now showing biomarker activity?
rationale: GFAP + sTREM2 both elevated = I

query: A 68-year-old patient and her family are deciding between continuing observation versus starting cholinesterase inhibitor therapy after a recent MCI diagnosis. They ask about typical 5-year progression rates and lifestyle adjustments. What is the central topic?
rationale: Clinical management + epidemiology + lifestyle, no biomarker = OTHER
